summary: Iris Murdoch discusses her approach to writing fiction, emphasizing the critical importance of plotting and structuring a novel entirely before writing the first sentence. She views the novel as an inherently tragicomic form that should reflect the terrible and funny nature of life, and she uses fiction to explore moral conflicts and the ambiguities of virtue rather than to preach philosophy.

principles.statement: Get the plot and structure right before you begin writing.
principles.rationale: You will be rewarded enormously by having the plot right, allowing you to see the entire story before writing the first sentence.
principles.source_quote: I think it's very important to get the structure right first to get the plot right and you'll be rewarded enormously by having the plot right

principles.statement: Philosophy should remain in the background of a novel.
principles.rationale: Novels should be realistic and focus on life and moral problems rather than being overtly philosophical treatises.
principles.source_quote: I don't want the philosophy to come in except in a very background and roundabout sense perhaps in relation to certain kinds of moral problem

principles.statement: Funniness should be everywhere in a novel.
principles.rationale: Life itself is both terrible and very funny, and the novel is essentially a tragicomic medium.
principles.source_quote: there's also the whole matter of the of the texture of the work of the funniness uh which I think should be everywhere in the novel

frameworks.name: Detailed Pre-Invention
frameworks.when_to_apply: When starting a new book, before writing the first sentence.
steps: Spend a great deal of time inventing the story.
Detail the invention of the plot.
Detail the invention of the characters.
Visualize the entire book before starting to write the prose.
frameworks.source_quote: I attach great importance to the detailed invention of the plot and the detail advention of the character so that I can see the whole thing before I start to write the first sentence

mental_models.name: The Tragicomic Novel
mental_models.description: Viewing the novel as an inherently mixed medium of tragedy and comedy, as opposed to theater which can sustain pure tragedy.
mental_models.example: Injecting 'funniness' everywhere in the texture of the work because life is both terrible and funny.

heuristics: See the whole thing before you start to write the first sentence.

signature_quotes.text: I'm a realistic writer I want to write about life and life is very terrible and very funny
signature_quotes.context: On her approach to realism in fiction.

signature_quotes.text: pure tragedy I think belongs to the theater the the novel is essentially tragic comic
signature_quotes.context: On the inherent tone and genre of the novel form.

signature_quotes.text: I don't want the philosophy to come in except in a very background and roundabout sense
signature_quotes.context: On being labeled a philosophical novelist.

anti_patterns.description: Writing pure tragedy in a novel.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Pure tragedy belongs to the theater; the novel form is essentially tragicomic and needs humor to reflect the texture of life.

anti_patterns.description: Starting to write the prose before fully inventing the plot and characters.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Without seeing the whole structure first, the writer misses out on the enormous rewards of a well-structured plot.

anti_patterns.description: Missing mundane, everyday details when writing from the perspective of a different gender.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It breaks the realism and creates pitfalls in character believability, such as forgetting that a male character needs to shave.
